## Comparing Two Means

-   In a one-sample *t*-test, we asked whether a sample mean differs
    from a known or hypothesized population mean.

-   Often, however, we do **not** have a known population mean.

-   Instead, we have **two samples**, each representing a different
    group.

-   Because these are samples, their means will **naturally differ** due
    to random sampling variation.

-   The key question:

    > Is the difference between the sample means **small enough to be
    > explained by sampling variation** if both groups come from the
    > same population?
    >
    > Or is the difference **too large to be plausibly explained by
    > chance alone**?

-   To answer this question, we test whether the two sample means could
    reasonably come from the **same underlying population mean**.

## Paired versus two independent samples

::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
Different statistical models are used depending on the relationship
between the two samples:

-   Paired comparison:

    -   Both treatments are applied to every sampled unit

-   Unpaired (independent) comparison:

    -   Each treatment group is composed of an independent, random
        sample of units

-   Paired designs are more powerful because they control for more
    extraneous variations between units
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
![Independent vs paired sampling
design](images/paired-vs-independent-samples.jpg){#fig-paired-vs-independent-samples
alt="Independent vs paired sampling design"}
:::
:::::

## Examples of paired and unpaired scenarios

::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
### Unpaired (indepedent) samples

- Two separate groups of individuals  
- Observations in one group are **not linked** to observations in the other

Examples:

-   Mean plant biomass in **fertilized vs. unfertilized plots**
-   Mean blood pressure in **patients receiving Drug A vs. Drug B**
-   Mean time spent hiding in cover for fish from **ponds with predators vs. ponds without predators**

:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
### Paired (dependent) samples

- Each observation in one group is **linked to a specific observation**
  in the other group
- Often the **same individual measured twice** or **matched pairs**

Examples:

-   Leaf nitrogen concentration in the **same plants before and after fertilization**
-   Blood pressure **before and after treatment** in the same patients
-   **Time spent hiding in cover** for the **same fish measured with and without predator cues** in an experimental tank
:::
:::::

## Comparing Two Means

::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
-   Many biological questions involve comparing the means of two groups.
-   Examples include treatment versus control or habitat comparisons.
-   Different study designs require different versions of the t test.
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![Distribution of bill length for Adelie and Gentoo penguins. Data from the Palmer Penguins dataset.](index_files/figure-revealjs/fig-penguin-bill-length-comparison-1.png){#fig-penguin-bill-length-comparison fig-alt='Two stacked histograms showing bill length distributions for Adelie and Gentoo penguins. Each panel shows counts of bill length measurements in millimeters.' width=768}
:::
:::

:::
:::::

## The [paired t test]{.keyword}

::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
1.  Used when observations occur in natural pairs such as before–after
    measurements.
2.  The analysis focuses on the differences within each pair.
3.  The mean of these differences is tested against zero.
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
![Paired measurements connected by lines illustrating before and after
observations. Source: Whitlock & Schluter
3e.](images/paired-t-test.png){#fig-paired-t-test
fig-alt="Pairs of points connected by lines representing before and after measurements for the same subjects."}
:::
:::::

## The [independent two-sample t test]{.keyword}

::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
1.  Used when observations in the two groups are independent.
2.  The test evaluates whether the difference between group means is
    greater than expected by chance.
3.  Common example is a treatment group compared with a control group.
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![Distribution of bill length for Adelie and Gentoo penguins. Red vertical lines mark the sample mean bill length for each species. Data from the Palmer Penguins dataset.](index_files/figure-revealjs/fig-penguin-bill-length-comparison-means-1.png){#fig-penguin-bill-length-comparison-means fig-alt='Two stacked histograms showing bill length distributions for Adelie and Gentoo penguins. Each panel shows counts of bill length measurements in millimeters, with a red vertical line indicating the mean bill length for that species.' width=768}
:::
:::

:::
:::::

## Choosing the Correct Test

::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
1.  The appropriate t test depends on the study design.
2.  One-sample tests compare a mean to a hypothesized value.
3.  Two-sample and paired tests compare means between groups.
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
![Decision tree for selecting one-sample, two-sample, or paired t tests.
Source: Whitlock & Schluter
3e.](images/t-test-decision-tree.png){#fig-t-test-decision-tree
fig-alt="Flowchart guiding the choice between one-sample, independent two-sample, and paired t tests."}
:::
:::::

<script>
  // htmlwidgets need to know to resize themselves when slides are shown/hidden.
  // Fire the "slideenter" event (handled by htmlwidgets.js) when the current
  // slide changes (different for each slide format).
  (function () {
    // dispatch for htmlwidgets
    function fireSlideEnter() {
      const event = window.document.createEvent("Event");
      event.initEvent("slideenter", true, true);
      window.document.dispatchEvent(event);
    }

    function fireSlideChanged(previousSlide, currentSlide) {
      fireSlideEnter();

      // dispatch for shiny
      if (window.jQuery) {
        if (previousSlide) {
          window.jQuery(previousSlide).trigger("hidden");
        }
        if (currentSlide) {
          window.jQuery(currentSlide).trigger("shown");
        }
      }
    }

    // hookup for slidy
    if (window.w3c_slidy) {
      window.w3c_slidy.add_observer(function (slide_num) {
        // slide_num starts at position 1
        fireSlideChanged(null, w3c_slidy.slides[slide_num - 1]);
      });
    }

  })();
</script>
